Notes

Notes

These are a collection of notes made for the purpose of storing knowledge and providing access to those that require the such.

The making of these notes starts with deriving proofs and equations by hand, and then rendering them beautifully in markdown. Hence, please do take your time to appreciate the effort, since making of each chapter or section of the notes is a not a trivial task.

That aside, please do enjoy browsing through the site, I really enjoy learning new things and posting about them online here. The mdbook package from the Rust Community happens to be a really useful tool to my journey due to it's very simplistic yet, visually pleasing sites.

NOTE: This website is not yet optimized for mobile devices, won't be doing so much that department. So, if encountering problems while browsing from phone, it is a sign that this is meant to be viewed on laptop or tablet at least.

Topics

  • Mathematics
  • Deep Learning

Mathematics

This whole section deals with mathematics (the adult version of it, PURE MATH). What is discussed here is pure mathematics, and things are discussed from scratch, with all the technical fluff in it.

A Short Intro

Mathematics is a very vast and a highly mature branch of science, to the point that right now, writing anything novel in maths is very rare and most of the times esoteric. Hence, what I will delve into are the very fundamental branches of mathematics which are tight with engineering, and develop those from an axiomatic standpoint.

This statement itself is enough to get me bashed online, but I believe in something called "facts are facts". By fundamental branches of mathematics, I mean those like Linear Algebra, Probability, Optimization and more. Technically, these three themselves drive more than 90% of the mathematics going into engineeering.

Secondly, if asked what does a pure math approach have to do with engineering, I believe that at some point, in one's learning journey, one should become familiar with the internal mechanics of the math that drives your systems. It comes in handy as an analysis tool, and maybe will help you design better systems.

NOTE: If you just plan on reading this through and hoping to understand everything, then please don't waste your time. Mathematics is not learnt by reading, it is learnt by practicing, writing down proofs, and solving problems. You are free to try otherwise, but you have been warned.

Please, at least try to derive the proofs by yourself before you go and click the drop-down menu to have a look at what I have done. Otherwise, this may very well be another one of those things you study for courses the night before and conveniently forget the same day after giving the exam.

Quick Links

  1. Linear Algebra Series

Deep Learning

This section deals with Deep Learning architectures. The goal as usual is to start from first principles, and quoting 3blue1brown, "I want to make it such that you could've come up with it yourself", or in simpler terms, reinventing the wheel.

A Short Intro

The central object of study in Deep Learning is: Neural Networks (NNs). These are special types of hypothesis functions, that are able to approximate any continuous function to any desired degree of accuracy, as stated by the Universal Approximation Theorem.

The good thing about deep learning is, underneath all the buzzwords floating around these days, core deep learning really boils down to some specific topology of networks and all you need to understand is why, what & how of those and the rest all branch off from those.

The goal is to not just understand one single network or one single topology, it is to understand the philosophy behind their design and what they are meant to achieve.

NOTE: I have emphasized on this point before, and I will do it again, but if you really want to understand the subject, please derive all the equations by hand, get that method of thinking into your skulls, and don't just memorize one proof.

No one will care if you can derive the backprop for an already well known model, but if you can optimize it for a new model out in the market, now that is something people will care about. Hence, don't treat this as a night before a college exam, and invest in the effort to understand things a level deeper. I have already done that, and this website is a testament to that.

Topics

  1. Deep Learning Architectures
  2. Deep Learning Theory

Deep Learning Architectures

This section simply deals with the different architectures and the problems they solve, mathematical techniques for analyzing the forward and backward pass, followed by converting them into algorithms and complexity analysis.

Chapters

  1. Multi-Layered Perceptron (MLP)

  2. Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)

Deep Learning Theory

Linear Algebra

Linear Algebra is the first fundamental pillar of mathematics that supports many engineering domains, primarily because many calculations can be reduced to matrix vector multiplication or matrix multiplications, making them significantly more easier to manipulate.

What makes it truly fundamental is, the tendency to linearize any system around a fixed point, or just linearizing as an approximation to make calculations and analysis more tractible inevitably imports the concepts from linear algebra into the picture.

From a pure math point of view, the fundamental object of study in Linear Algebra is: Vector Space. Everything in linear algebra revolves around vector spaces including the fact that vector spaces with one interesting property, automatically becomes amenable to computation and constructive proofs inevitably become algorithms. Hopefully, for people thinking, "another theory with no use", this sparks some motivation.

The first few chapters are following the progression of Linear Algebra Done Right, Sheldon Axler, but the content although inspired from that, has alot more than what is presented in the book.

Chapters

  1. Vector Spaces
    References:

    • Linear Algebra Done Right, Sheldon Axler

  2. Finite Dimensional Vector Spaces
    References:

    • Linear Algebra Done Right, Sheldon Axler

  3. Linear Maps
    References:

    • Linear Algebra Done Right, Sheldon Axler

1. Vector Spaces

Before delving into what a vector space is, it is necessary to see what are the components that make a vector space.

1.1. Fields

Definition 1.1 [Fields]

A collection of objects F endowed with operations +,⋅ (addition and multiplication respectively) denoted as F≡(F,+,⋅) is a field if it satisfies the following conditions:

  1. [Closure]: ∀a,b∈F

    1. a+b∈F
    2. a⋅b∈F

  2. [Commutativity]: ∀a,b∈F

    1. a+b=b+a
    2. a⋅b=b⋅a

  3. [Associativity]: ∀a,b,c∈F

    1. a+(b+c)=(a+b)+c
    2. a⋅(b⋅c)=(a⋅b)⋅c

  4. [Identities]: ∃0,1∈F such that:

    1. ∀a∈F,a+0=a
    2. ∀a∈F,a⋅1=a

  5. [Inverses]:

    1. ∀a∈F,∃−a∈F:a+(−a)=0
    2. ∀a∈F−{0},∃a−1:a⋅a−1=1

  6. [Distributivity]: ∀a,b,c∈F,

    • a⋅(b+c)=a⋅b+a⋅c

Each property is important and the loss of even one of them will mean NOT a field.

Common examples of fields are:

  1. Rational Numbers Q under usual +,⋅
  2. Real Numbers R under usual +,⋅
  3. Complex Numbers C under usual +,⋅
  4. Boolean Field F2​≡({0,1},⊕,⋅) where (⊕,⋅) are XOR & AND operations respectively.

Properties of a Field [P-1.1]

In all the properties, F≡(F,+,⋅) is the given field.

1. [Uniqueness of Identity Elements]

  1. ∃!0∈F, meaning there is a unique 0∈F
  2. ∃!1∈F, meaning there is a unique 1∈F
Proof

1.1 Suppose there are two zero elements (additive identities), call them 01​,02​:01​=02​.

​​∀a∈F,​​a+01​=a⟶(s1)a+02​=a⟶(s2)​By definition 1.1.4.1, additive identityHence,​02​+01​=02​, sub. 02​ in (s1)01​+02​=01​, sub. 01​ in (s2)​⟶(s3)But 02​+01​=01​+02​→Definition 1.1.2.1, commutativity of +∴01​=02​→from (s3)​

This contradicts the non-uniqueness of additive identity.
Meaning, the additive identity is unique and ∃!0∈Fq.e.d

1.2 Suppose there are two one elements (multiplicative identities), call them 11​,12​:11​=12​.

​​∀a∈F,​​a⋅11​=a⟶(s1)a⋅12​=a⟶(s2)​By definition 1.1.4.2, multiplicative identityHence,​12​⋅11​=12​, sub. 12​ in (s1)11​⋅12​=11​, sub. 11​ in (s2)​⟶(s3)But 12​⋅11​=11​⋅12​→Definition 1.1.2.2, commutativity of ⋅∴11​=12​→from (s3)​

This contradicts the non-uniqueness of multiplicative identity.
Meaning, the multiplicative identity is unique and ∃!1∈Fq.e.d

2. [Uniqueness of Inverses]

  1. ∀a∈F,∃!−a∈F:a+(−a)=0, i.e. additive inverse is unique
  2. ∀a∈F−{0},∃!a−1:a⋅a−1=1, i.e. multiplicative inverse is unique
Proof

2.1 Suppose for any a∈F there are two additive inverses call them i1​,i2​:i1​=i2​.

​​a+i1​=0⟶(s1)a+i2​=0⟶(s2)​By definition 1.1.5.1, additive inverse​∴i1​=i1​+0,∴i1​=i1​+(a+i2​),∴i1​=(i1​+a)+i2​,∴i1​=(a+i1​)+i2​,∴i1​=0+i2​,∴i1​=i2​+0,∴i1​=i2​,​​ definition 1.1.4.1, additive identity  by (s2)  definition 1.1.3.1, associativity of + definition 1.1.2.1, commutativity of + by (s1)  definition 1.1.2.1, commutativity of + definition 1.1.4.1, additive identity​​

This contradicts the non-uniqueness of additive inverse.
Meaning, the additive inverse is unique, i.e.
∀a∈F ∃!−a∈F:a+(−a)=0q.e.d

2.2 Suppose ∀a∈F−{0}, there are two multiplcative inverses, call them m1​,m2​:m1​=m2​.

​​a⋅m1​=1⟶(s1)a⋅m2​=1⟶(s2)​By definition 1.1.5.2, multiplicative inverse​∴m1​=m1​⋅1,∴m1​=m1​⋅(a⋅m2​),∴m1​=(m1​⋅a)⋅m2​,∴m1​=(a⋅m1​)⋅m2​,∴m1​=1⋅m2​,∴m1​=m2​⋅1,∴m1​=m2​,​​ definition 1.1.4.2, multiplicative identity  by (s2)  definition 1.1.3.2, associativity of ⋅ definition 1.1.2.2, commutativity of ⋅ by (s1)  definition 1.1.2.2, commutativity of ⋅ definition 1.1.4.2, multiplicative identity​​

This contradicts the non-uniqueness of multiplicative inverse.
Meaning, the multiplicative inverese is unique, i.e.
∀a∈F−{0},∃!a−1∈F:a⋅a−1=1,q.e.d

3. [Inverse of Inverse]

  1. ∀a∈F,−(−a)=a
  2. ∀a∈F−{0},(a−1)−1=a
Proof

3.1 By definition 1.1.5.1, ∀a∈F,∃−a∈F such that:

∴∴​a+(−a)=0(−a)+a=0,−(−a)=a,​​→ definition 1.1.2.1,commutativity of +→ definition 1.1.5.1, additive inverse​

So, a is a possible additive inverse for −a. P-1.1.2.1 solidifies the uniqueness of a as additive inverse for −a. q.e.d

3.2 By definition 1.1.5.2, ∀a∈F−{0},∃a−1∈F such that:

∴∴​a⋅a−1=1a−1⋅a=1,(a−1)−1=a,​​→ definition 1.1.2.2, commutativity of ⋅→ definition 1.1.5.2, multiplicative inverse​

So, a is a possible multiplicative inverse for a−1. P-1.1.2.2 solidifies the uniqueness of a as multiplicative inverse for a−1. q.e.d

4. [Cancellation Law]

  1. ∀a,b,x∈F,a+x=b⟹x=(−a)+b
  2. ∀b,x∈F,a∈F−{0},a⋅x=b⟹x=a−1⋅b
Proof

4.1 Given a+x=b⟶(s1),

​then, ∃−a∈F:a+(−a)=0∴(−a)+a=0∴(−a)+(a+x)=(−a)+b∴0+x=(−a)+b∴x=(−a)+b q.e.d​​→ Definition 1.1.5.1, additive inverse→ Def. 1.1.2.1, Commutativity of +→(s2)→ Adding −a in (s1) on both sides→ (s2) & Def. 1.1.3.1, Associativity of +→ Definition 1.1.4.1, additive inverse &  Definition 1.1.2.1, commutativity of +​

4.2 Given a⋅x=b with a=0, ⟶(s1)

​then, ∃a−1∈F:a⋅a−1=1∴a−1⋅a=1∴a−1⋅(a⋅x)=(−a)⋅b∴1⋅x=a−1⋅b∴x=a−1⋅b q.e.d​​→ Definition 1.1.5.2, multiplicative inverse→ Def. 1.1.2.2, Commutativity of ⋅​(s2)→ Multiply a−1 in (s1) on both sides→ (s2) & Def. 1.1.3.2, Associativity of ⋅→ Definition 1.1.4.2, multiplicative inverse &  Definition 1.1.2.2, commutativity of ⋅​

5. [Inverse on Operations]

  1. ∀a,b∈F,−(a+b)=(−b)+(−a)
  2. ∀a,b∈F−{0},(a⋅b)−1=b−1⋅a−1
Proof

5.1 Given a,b∈F, consider x=(−b)+(−a),

∴∴∴∴∴​(a+b)+x=a+(b+x)(a+b)+x=a+[b+((−b)+(−a))](a+b)+x=a+[(b+(−b))+(−a)](a+b)+x=a+[0+(−a)](a+b)+x=a+(−a)(a+b)+x=0​​→ Def. 1.1.3.1, Associativity of +→ expanding x→ Def. 1.1.3.1, Associativity of +→ Def. 1.1.5.1, Additive Inverse→ Def. 1.1.2.1, Commutativity of + & Def. 1.1.4.1, Additive Identity→ Def. 1.1.4.1, Additive Identity⟶ (s3)​

Since x acts an additive inverse by (s3) & Def. 1.1.5.1, we have x=(−b)+(−a) as a bonafide addtive inverse for (a+b) q.e.d

5.2 Given a,b∈F−{0}, consider x=b−1⋅a−1,

∴∴∴∴∴​(a⋅b)⋅x=a⋅(b⋅x)(a⋅b)⋅x=a⋅[b⋅(b−1⋅a−1)](a⋅b)⋅x=a⋅[(b⋅b−1)⋅a−1](a⋅b)⋅x=a⋅[1⋅a−1](a⋅b)⋅x=a⋅a−1(a⋅b)⋅x=1​​→ Def. 1.1.3.2, Associativity of ⋅→ expanding x→ Def. 1.1.3.1, Associativity of ⋅→ Def. 1.1.5.2, Multiplicative Inverse→ Def. 1.1.2.2, Commutativity of ⋅& Def. 1.1.4.2, Multiplicative Identity→ Def. 1.1.4.2, Multiplicative Identity⟶ (s3)​

Since x acts an multiplicative inverse by (s3) & Def. 1.1.5.2, we have x=b−1⋅a−1 as a bonafide multiplicative inverse for (a⋅b) q.e.d

6. [Multiplication with 0]

  • ∀a∈F,a⋅0=0
Proof

​let a∈F∴​a⋅0=a⋅(0+0)a⋅0=a⋅0+a⋅0​​⟶ Def. 1.1.4.1, Additive Inverse⟶ Def. 1.1.6, Distributivity of + over ⋅​Adding −(a⋅0) on both sides, which exists by Def. 1.1.5.1,∴​a⋅0+[−(a⋅0)]=(a⋅0+a⋅0)+[−(a⋅0)]​∴∴∴​0=a⋅0+(a⋅0+[−(a⋅0)])0=a⋅0+00=a⋅0​​⟶ Def. 1.1.5.1, Addtive Inverse on LHS & Def. 1.1.3.1, Associativity of + in RHS⟶ Def 1.1.5.1, Additive Inverse in RHS⟶ Def. 1.1.4.1, Additive Inverse​​

Hence, multiplying anything with 0 yields 0 itself  q.e.d

Elaborating on the last step

7. [Multiplication with -1]

  • ∀a∈F,−1⋅a=−a
Proof

From P-1.1.6, multiplication with 0, ∀a∈F, a⋅0=0

∴∴∴∴∴​a⋅(1+(−1))=0a⋅1+a⋅(−1)=0a⋅1+−1⋅a=0a+−1⋅a=0−a=−1⋅a​​⟶ Def. 1.1.5.1, Additive Inverse⟶ Def. 1.1.6, Distributivity⟶ Def. 1.1.2.2, Commutativity of ⋅⟶ Def. 1.1.4.2, Multiplicative Identity⟶ Def. 1.1.5.1, Additive Inverse of a​

Elaborating on the last step of the proof, Def. 1.1.5.1 tells us that −1⋅a is a valid additive inverse of a and P-1.1.2.1 solidifies the uniqueness of that.

 q.e.d

8. [Inequality of 0 & 1]

  • For any field F≡(F,+,⋅), 0=1 always!
Proof

This is a proof by contradiction, so let's start by assuming that 0=1.

​Hence, ∀a∈F,∴​a⋅0=a⋅10=a​​⟶P-1.1.6, Multiplication with 0 in LHS & Def. 1.1.4.2, Multiplicative Identity in LHS​This straight off just contradicts the assumption that a=0which can only mean that our initial assumption of 0=1is wrong!Hence, 0=1 for any fieldq.e.d​

9. [Reason for 0 not having Multiplicative Inverse]

  • 0 cannot have a multiplicative inverse in any field
Proof

Let's suppose 0 did have a multiplcative inverse, call it 0−1

​∴&∴​0⋅0−1=10⋅0−1=00=1​​⟶ Def. 1.1.5.2, Multiplicative Inverse⟶ P-1.1.6, multiplication with 0​ This directly contradicts P-1.1.8, hence, 0−1 cannot exist. q.e.d​

The point with all these properties is, you don't need to remember them. Almost all of them are something which is true in rationals, reals and complex numbers. But the point is, they apply to all fields.

Like, multiplication with 0 and -1, commonly used everywhere, is a general property of fields, not just a special case. The not so obvious and theoretical ones are last two, which are inequality of the identity elements. Because of this one property, any field must at least have two elements 0 & 1. That smallest field is F2​ which has only 0,1 as elements and satisfies field definition under XOR and AND as the two operations.

This ends the party with fields, next is the main show, which is Vector Spaces.

1.2 Vector Spaces

Definition 1.2 [Vector Space]

A collection of objects V endowed with operations +,⋅ (vector addition and scalar multiplication respectively), over a field F≡(F,+^​,⋅^) denoted as V(F)≡(V,+,⋅,F) is a vector space if it satisfies the following conditions:

  1. [Closure]: ∀u,v∈V, c∈F

    1. u+v∈V
    2. c⋅v∈V

  2. [Commutativity]: ∀u,v∈V, a∈F

    1. u+v=v+u
    2. c⋅v=v⋅c

  3. [Associativity]: ∀u,v,w∈V & a,b∈F

    1. u+(v+w)=(v+u)+w
    2. a⋅(b⋅v)=(a⋅^b)⋅v

  4. [Identities]

    1. ∃0V​:∀v∈V,v+0V​=v
    2. ∀v∈V,1F​⋅v=v,  where 1F​ is the mult. identity from F

  5. [Inverses]

    • ∀v∈V,∃−v∈V :v+(−v)=0V​

  6. [Distributivity]: ∀u,v∈V & a,b∈F

    1. a⋅(u+v)=a⋅u+ a⋅v
    2. (a+^​b)⋅v=a⋅v+b⋅v

Six conditions, just like fields, with some minor changes. This definition fits any abstract thing you might choose to call a vector.

The second thing is, the definition of commutativity differs from the standard textbook definition. I define it this way because I respect symmetry 😉.

Common examples of vector spaces are:

  1. Fn under elementwise addition ⊕ & elementwise scalar multiplication ⊙ is a vector space over F≡(F,+^​,⋅^). Usually F=R,C,Q,{0,1} are encountred in Physics, Coding Theory, Deep Learning or any other domain using vectors.

  2. Pm​={∑i=0m​ai​⋅zi: ai​∈F∀i=0,1,...,m}, the set of all polynomials with degree less than or equal to m, and coefficients from F, with usual addition and scalar multiplication (+,⋅) is a vector space over F≡(F,+^​,⋅^).

  3. FX≡F(X,F), the set of all possible function from a non-empty set X to a set F under pointwise addition + & pointwise multiplication ⋅ is a vector space over F≡(F,+^​,⋅^).

Among these, the first one is the most important because it is the most amenable to computation.

Properties of Vector Spaces [P-1.2]

V(F)≡(V,+,⋅,F) is a vector space over F≡(F,+^​,⋅^)

1. [Uniqueness of Additive Identity]

  • 0V​ is unique in any vector space V(F)
Proof

Supposing there are two additive inverses, call them 01​, 02​, such that, 01​=02​ then,

∴&∴​01​=01​+02​02​=02​+01​01​+02​=02​+01​01​=02​​​⟶ Def. 1.2.4.1, Additive Inverse 02​⟶ Def. 1.2.4.1, Additive Inverse 01​​ (s1) ⟶ Def. 1.2.2.1, Commutativity of +⟶ From (s1)​

This contradicts the non-uniqueness assumed at the start of the proof.

Hence, additive inverse, 0V​ in the whole vector space is unique. q.e.d

2. [Uniqueness of Additive Inverse]

  • ∀v∈V,−v: v+(−v)=0V​ is unique.
Proof

Suppose, for a v∈V, it has two additive inverses, call them i1​ & i2​, which are not equal. Then,

∴∴∴∴∴∴​i1​=i1​+0V​i1​=i1​+(v+i2​)i1​=(i1​+v)+i2​i1​=(v+i1​)+i2​i1​=0V​+i2​i1​=i2​+0V​i1​=i2​​​⟶ Def. 1.2.4.1, Addtive Identity⟶ Def. 1.2.5, Additive Inverse i2​⟶ Def. 1.2.3.1, Associativity of +⟶ Def. 1.2.2.1, Commutativity of +⟶ Def. 1.2.5, Additive Inverse i1​⟶ Def. 1.2.2.1, Commutativity of +⟶ Def. 1.2.4.1, Additive Identity​

This contradicts the initial assumption of the proof that the two were not equal.

Meaning, the additive inverse for any v∈V is unique!  q.e.d

3. [Cancellation laws for Vectors]

∀u,v,x∈V and c∈F−{0F​}, where 0F​ is the additive identity of the underlying field F,

  1. u+x=v⟹ x=−u+v =v+(−u)
  2. c⋅x=v⟹ x=c−1⋅v
Proof

3.1 u+x=v given.

∴∴∴∴​−u+(u+x)=−u+v(−u+u)+x=−u+v0V​+x=−u+vx=−u+v=v+(−u)​​⟶ Adding −u both sides⟶ Def. 1.2.3.1, Associativity of +⟶ Def. 1.2.2.1, Commutativty of + & Def. 1.2.5, Additive Inverse, togehter⟶ Def. 1.2.2.1, Commutativity of + &  Def. 1.2.4.1, Additive Identity (LHS only)​

q.e.d

3.2 c⋅x=v given (with proper assumptions on the variables).

∴∴∴∴​c−1⋅(c⋅x)=c−1⋅v(c−1⋅^c)⋅x=c−1v1F​⋅x=c−1⋅vx=c−1⋅v​​⟶ Multiply c−1 both sides⟶ Def. 1.2.3.2, Associativity of ⋅→ Def. 1.1.2.2, Commutativity of ⋅^ & Def. 1.1.5.2, Multiplicative Inverse⟶ Def. 1.2.4.2, Identity for scalar mult.​

 q.e.d

4. [Multiplication with Zero]

  1. a⋅0V​=0V​,∀a∈F
  2. 0F​⋅v=0V​,∀v∈V
Proof

4.1

∴∴∴∴​a⋅0V​=a⋅(0V​+0V​)a⋅0V​=a⋅0V​+a⋅0V​a⋅0V​+[−(a⋅0V​)]=(a⋅0V​+a⋅0V​)+[−(a⋅0V​)]0V​=0V​+a⋅0V​0V​=a⋅0V​​​⟶ Def. 1.2.4.1, Additive Identity⟶ Def. 1.2.6.1, Distributivity⟶ Adding −(a⋅0V​) both sides⟶ Def. 1.2.3.1, Associativity of + in RHS & Def. 1.2.5, Additive Inverse⟶ Def. 1.2.4.1, Additive Identity​ q.e.d

4.2

∴∴∴∴​0F​⋅v=(0F​+^​0F​)⋅v0F​⋅v=0F​⋅v+0F​⋅v0F​⋅v+[−(0F​⋅v)]=(0F​⋅v+0F​⋅v)+[−(0F​⋅v)]0V​=0V​+0F​⋅v0V​=0F​⋅v​​⟶ Def. 1.1.4.1, Add. Id. in Fields⟶ Def. 1.2.6.2, Distributivity⟶ Adding −(0F​⋅v) both sides⟶ Def. 1.2.3.1, Associativity of + & Def. 1.2.5, Additive Inverse⟶ Def. 1.2.4.1, Additive Identity​ q.e.d

5. [Multiplication with −1F​]

  • (−1F​)⋅v=−v,∀v∈V
Proof

∴∴∴∴​0F​⋅v=0V​(1F​+^​(−1F​))⋅v=0V​0V​=1F​⋅v+(−1F​)⋅v0V​=v+(−1F​)⋅v(−1F​)⋅v=−v​​⟶ P-1.2.4.2, multiplcation with zero⟶ Def. 1.1.5.1, Additive Inverse in Fields⟶ Def. 1.2.6.2, Distributivity⟶ Def. 1.2.4.2, Identity for scalar mult.⟶ Def. 1.2.5, Additive Inverse​

q.e.d

6. [Inverse of Inverse]

  • ∀v∈V,−(−v)=v
Proof

Suppose v∈V.

Since −v is the additive inverse of v,

∴∴​v+(−v)=0V​(−v)+v=0V​​​⟶ Def. 1.2.5, Additive Inverse⟶ Def. 1.2.2.1, Commutativity of +​

The above statement shows that v is an additive inverse of −v, Def. 1.2.5.

∴​−(−v)=v​

q.e.d

1.3 Subspaces

Definition 1.3 [Subspace]

  1. Given V(F) is a vector space, any U ⊂V is a subspace of V, if U is a vector space over F under the same vector addition and scalar multiplication as V(F).

    It is denoted as U(F)≤V(F) ≡U≤V.

  2. U is a proper subspace of V if U⊊V and U(F) ≤V(F), i.e. U is strictly contained within V and is a vector space in itself.

    It is denoted as U(F)<V(F) ≡U<V.

The reason for subspace will become pretty clear after a property. The concept of subspace reduces alot of effort in proving something is a bonafide vector space.

Physically, the concept of subspace reduces the no.of components that is required to represent a vector. Principle Component Analysis (PCA) is this exact line of thought put into action.

The whole concept of Eigenvalues rest upon your understanding of subspaces. So, better know how to use this.

Properties of Subspaces [P-1.3]

V(F)≡(V,+,⋅,F) is a vector space over F≡(F,+^​,⋅^)

1. [Alternate Defintion of Subspace]

  • U≤V iff:
    1. U⊂V
    2. U is closed under (+,⋅)
Proof

(⟹) If U≤V, then P-1.3.1.1 & P-1.3.1.2 are both valid, by Def. 1.3.1, Subspace & Def. 1.2.1, Closure.

That's why this part is just free marks.

(⟸) Say, U⊂V, first point assumed. By the virtue of elements of U belonging to V,

  1. [Commutativity] ∀u,v∈U & c∈F,

    • u+v=v+u
    • c⋅v=v⋅c

  2. [Associativity]: ∀u,v,w∈U & a,b∈F

    • u+(v+w)=(v+u)+w
    • a⋅(b⋅v)=(a⋅^b)⋅v

  3. [Identities]

    • ∀v∈U,1F​⋅v=v,  where 1F​ is the mult. identity from F

  4. [Distributivity]: ∀u,v∈U & a,b∈F

    • a⋅(u+v)=a⋅u+ a⋅v
    • (a+^​b)⋅v=a⋅v+b⋅v

    These properties are inherited directly by virtue of U⊂V, Def. 1.2, Vector Space. Some conditions are not yet fulfilled which is where closure comes in.

    Now, say U is closed under (+,⋅). Then,

  5. [Closure]: ∀u,v∈U and c∈F

    • u+v∈U
    • c⋅v∈U

    Now, closure brings in the remaining too:

  • 0F​⋅u=0V​∈U, which comes about due to closure in U & P-1.2.4.2, multiplication with 0.

  • −1F​⋅u=−u∈U which comes about due to closure in U & P-1.2.5, multiplication with -1.

Hence, by Def. 1.2, Vector Space & Def 1.3.1, subspace, U≤V q.e.d

2. [Intersection of Subspaces]

  • Let U,W≤V, then U∩W≤V,

    i.e. intersection of two subspaces is also a subspace.

Proof

If U,W≤V, then by Def. 1.3.1, U,W⊂V, hence U∩W⊂V.

  1. If u,v∈U∩W, then u,v∈U & u,v∈W, by definition of intersection.

    Hence, since U,W are subspaces, then by Def. 1.2.1.1, closure, u+v∈U & u+v∈W ⟹ u+v∈U∩W

  2. If v∈U∩W, then v∈U & v∈W, by definition of intersection.

    Hence, ∀c∈F,c⋅v∈U & c⋅v∈W by Def. 1.3.1 & Def. 1.2.1.1.

    ⟹c⋅v∈U∩W.

Hence, U∩W satisfies closure.

By P-1.3.1, U∩W is a subspace of V q.e.d

3. [Union of Subspaces]

  • U,W≤V. Then U∪W is a subspace iff U⊂W or W⊂U
Proof

(⟸) If one of the subspaces is contained within the other, then U∪W=U or W which are given as subspaces.

Hence, this is free marks.

(⟹) This is the non-intuitive part. Let u,v∈U∪W⟹ u,v∈U or W.
4 cases arise here:

  1. u,v∈U, closure guaranteed here since U is a subspace, given [+ Def. 1.2.1]
  2. u,v∈W, closure guaranteed here since W is a subspace, given [+ Def. 1.2.1]
  3. u∈U,v∈W−U, closure cannot be guaranteed here, other than the case where W⊂U, which becomes vacuously true since v∈ϕ.
  4. u∈U−W,v∈W, closure cannot be guaranteed here, other than the case where U⊂W, which becomes vacuously true since u∈ϕ.

Hence, U∪W being a valid subspace implies U⊂W or W⊂U q.e.d

4. [Intersection is the largest contained subspace]

Given U,V≤V, U∩W is the largest subspace contained in both U & W.

Proof

P-1.3.2 already shows that U∩W is a subspace.

Now let X≤V be a subspace s.t. it is contained in both U,W.
Hence, X≤U,X≤W ⟹, X⊂U,X⊂W. , by P-1.3.1
Hence, X⊂U∩W, by definition of intersection.
Since X is also a subspace, by construction of the proof,
X≤U∩W, by Def. 1.3.1 q.e.d.

1.4 Subset Sum

P-1.3.3 leaves you hanging on a question, given two subspaces, if intersection can be used to find smaller subspaces [P-1.3.2], then how do we create bigger subspaces?

The answer to that is: Subset Sum!

Definition 1.4 [Subset Sum]

Given subspaces of V(F), U1​,U2​,...,Un​≤V:

  1. U1​+U2​+...+Un​≡∑i=1n​Ui​ ≜{u1​+u2​+...+un​: ui​∈Ui​∀i=1,2,...n}

  2. If every v∈∑i=1n​Ui​, ∃!ui​,i=1,2,...,n:v=∑i=1n​ui​, then ∑i=1n​Ui​ is a direct sum and is then denoted by ⨁i=1n​Ui​.

This looks like one of those esoteric things, that just makes a cameo in some analyses and just doesn't come back again, but this is much more than that.

The use case comes about when instead of looking at adding up some given subspaces, you start looking at how a given subspace can be broken down into a subset sum (much better, a direct sum).

For example [looking into the future for motivation here]:

  1. Eigenspaces & Matrix Diagonalization hinge upon decomposition of columns space of matrix into a direct sum.

  2. Quantum Mechanics frequently uses decomposition of particle states into direct sums to separate out useful information from them.

  3. Fourier Analysis & Signal Processing is literally decomposing a signal space into sum of subspaces where signals of a single frequency live. Most basic use case of subset sum.

Properties of Subset Sum [P-1.4]

V(F)≡(V,+,⋅,F) is a vector space over F≡(F,+^​,⋅^)

1. [Sum of Subspaces is a smallest containing subspace]

Given subspaces U1​,U2​,...,Un​≤V, ∑i=1n​Ui​ is the smallest subspace containing all the Ui​'s.

Proof

​S≡i=1∑n​Ui​={i=1∑n​ui​:ui​∈Ui​,i=1,2,...,n}​​⟶ Def. 1.4.1, Subset Sum​∴​Since all vectors from Ui​’s also belong to V,by Def. 1.3.1, all vectors in S also come inside V by repeated application of Def. 1.2.1.1,closure of +.Hence, S⊂V.⟶(s1)∀u,v∈S,They can be represented as:​∴∴∴∴​u=i=1∑n​xi​, where xi​∈Ui​v=i=1∑n​yi​, where yi​∈Ui​Hence, u+v=i=1∑n​xi​+i=1∑n​yi​u+v=i=1∑n​(xi​+yi​)xi​+yi​∈Ui​,∀iu+v∈S∀c∈F,c⋅v=c⋅i=1∑n​yi​c⋅v=i=1∑n​(c⋅yi​)c⋅yi​∈Ui​,∀ic⋅v∈S​​⟶Def. 1.4.1, Subset Sum⟶Def. 1.4.1, Subset Sum⟶ Def. 1.2.3.1, Associativity of + applied repeatedly⟶ Def. 1.2.1.1, Closure under +⟶ Def. 1.4.1 applied to S→(s2)⟶ Def. 1.2.6.1, Distributivity applied repeatedly⟶ Def. 1.2.1.2, Closure under ⋅⟶ Def. 1.4.1 applied to S→ (s3)​​Hence, from (s1), (s2), (s3) and P-1.3.1, S is a valid subspace q.e.d​

2. [Condition for Direct Sum]

A subset sum ∑i=1n​Ui​ is a direct sum iff 0V​ can be written uniquely as sum of all zeros from each of the Ui​'s.

Proof

(⟹) If S≡∑i=1n​Ui​ is a direct sum, then by Def. 1.4.2, 0V​ has to be uniqely decomposable and since summation of zero vectors always yileds the zero vector, Def. 1.2.4.1, that must be the unique combination.

Hence, this is free marks.

(⟸) Suppose 0V​ is uniquely decomposable as sum of all zeros only. Then let's assume that some v∈ S can be written in two ways:

v=∑i=1n​xi​=∑i=1n​yi​
where xi​'s and yi​'s are not all equal.

∴∴∴∴∴∴∴​v+(−v)=0V​i=1∑n​xi​−i=1∑n​yi​=0V​i=1∑n​xi​+i=1∑n​(−yi​)=0V​i=1∑n​xi​+(−yi​)=0V​xi​,−yi​∈Ui​,∀ixi​+(−yi​)∈Ui​,∀ixi​+(−yi​)=0V​xi​=yi​​​⟶ Def. 1.2.5, Additive Inverse⟶ Valid additive inverse⟶ Def. 1.2.3.1, Associativity of + applied repeatedly⟶ Def. 1.4.1 & Def. 1.2.5, Additive inverse⟶ Def. 1.2.1.1, Closure of +⟶ Uniqueness of 0V​ in S⟶ P-1.2.6, inverse of inverse​

This contradicts the non-equalness of the two assumed at start of the proof. Which means, any vector in S must be uniquely decomposable into a sum of vectors from each individual subspace. q.e.d

3. [Direct Sum Condition for two subspaces]

Let U,W≤V
U+W is a direct sum iff U∩W={0V​}

Proof

(⟹) Suppose U+W is a direct sum. Then,

​0V​=v+(−v),v∈U∩W​​⟶ Def. 1.2.5, Addtive inverse​​But since U+W is a direct sum, v∈U=0V​&−v∈W=0V​​​⟶ P-1.4.2, direct sum means  unique decomposition of 0V​​​The v∈U&−v∈W argument is valid since both belong to U∩WHence, v∈U∩W=0V​∀v∈U∩W⟹U∩W={0V​} q.e.d​

(⟸) Say, U∩W={0V​}

Since, 0V​∈U+W≡S, P-1.4.1, subset sum is a subspace  + Def. 1.2.4.1, Additive identity,

∃u∈U,w∈W :u+w=0V​

∴∴​u=−w∈Wu∈U∩WHence, u=0V​since, U∩W={0V​}​⟶ Def. 1.2.5, Additive Inverse⟶ Definition of intersection

Hence, 0V​∈S can only be represented by the zero sum, hence by P-1.4.2, U+W is a direct sum.  q.e.d

1.5 Lattices

In the last sections, we discovered the intersection operation which allows us to create the largest contained subspace in a list of subspaces P-1.3.4 + Induction, and the subset sum operation which creates the smallest containing subspace, P-1.4.1.

Notice that these form a greatest lower bound (glb) and least upper bound (lub) operations on subspaces, respectively. But lub and glb require some sort of ordering, which is what is provided by subspace inclusion (≤).

Hence, the collection of all the subspaces of a vector space V(F), call it S(V) ≡{U:U≤V} forms a special structure with the subspace inclusion, called a Lattice.

There are two definitions of lattice, one algebraic and one from Order Theory. We'll show that under both of them, S(V) is a lattice. Even otherwise, both the definitions of lattice are equivalent. This is just a small analysis, usually not taught in a linear algebra course.

Definition 1.5 [Lattice]

  1. [Parital Ordering] Given a set S, a binary relation ≤ is called a partial ordering on S if it satisfies the following conditions:

    1. [Reflexivity] ∀x∈S,x≤x
    2. [Antisymmetry] ∀x,y∈S, x≤y,y≤x⟹x=y
    3. [Transistivity] ∀x,y,z∈S, x≤y,y≤z⟹x≤z

    And (S,≤) is called a Poset [Set with partial ordering].

  2. [Lattice: Order Theory] A poset (S,≤) is a lattice if there exist two operations, defined for all possible a,b∈S:

    1. ∧ (glb, infimum or meet): The Greatest Lower Bound of a,b∈S,a∧b is the largest element in S that is smaller than a,b.

    2. ∨ (lub, supremum or join): The Least Upper Bound of a,b∈S,a∨b is the smallest element in S that is larger than both a,b.

  3. [Lattice: Algebraic] A collection of objects S endowed with two operations meet(∧) and join(∨) forms a lattice (S,∧,∨) if it satisfies the following:

    1. [Closure]: ∀a,b∈S,
      1. a∧b∈S
      2. a∨b∈S
    2. [Commutativity] ∀a,b∈S,
      1. a∧b=b∧a
      2. a∨b=b∨a
    3. [Associativity] ∀a,b,c∈S,
      1. a∧(b∧c)=(a∧b)∧c
      2. a∨(b∨c)=(a∨b)∨c
    4. [Idempotence] ∀a∈S
      1. a∧a=a
      2. a∨a=a
    5. [Absorption] ∀a,b∈S
      1. a∧(a∨b)=a
      2. a∨(a∧b)=a

Lattices also seem like one of those esoteric things that make a cameo and then never show their faces again, but look at the examples below and think again.

Examples:

  1. Boolean Logic: ({0,1},∧,∨) where ∧,∨ denote logical AND & logical OR respectively forms a lattice. Hence, the whole foundations of digital electronics and the building of digital circuits is grounded in Lattice theory.

  2. Power Set X is a set. (P(X),∩,∪) forms a lattice, where P(X) is the power set of X. This is one of the simplest examples of algebraic lattices. The ordering that one can verify is subset inclusion.

  3. Divisibility Lattice (Z+​,gcd,lcm) forms an algebraic lattice with usual ordering ≤.

Alot of the simple things in life form a lattice. It is one of those hidden structures you need to put some effort into finding.

Properties of Lattices [P-1.5]

V(F)≡(V,+,⋅,F) is a vector space over F≡(F,+^​,⋅^)

1. [Collection of Subspaces form an order theoretic lattice]

Given S(V)≜{U:U≤V}
(S,≤) forms an order theoretic lattice, where ≤ is subspace inclusion.

Proof

Firstly, to prove that (S,≤) is a poset.

​​1.[Reflexivity]:∀U∈S,U≤U since every subspace is a subspace of itself​​2.[Antisymmetry]:​​U,W∈SU≤W⟹U⊂W⟶ Def. 1.3.1 of subspaceW≤U⟹W⊂U⟶ Def. 1.3.1 of subspace​∴U=W, by antisymmetry of subset inclusion3.[Transistivity]:U,W,X∈S∴​U≤W⟹U⊂WW≤X⟹W⊂XU⊂X⟹U≤X​​⟶ by Def. 1.3.1, subspace⟶ by Def. 1.3.1, subspace⟶ Since both are subspaces by construction​Hence, by Def. 1.5.1, (S,≤) is a poset.The operations + & ∩ serve as lub and glb resp. by P-1.4.1 & P-1.3.4∴(S,≤) is a lattice by Def. 1.5.2 q.e.d​

2. [Class of Subspaces form an Algebraic Lattice]

Given S(V)={U:U≤V}, the structure (S(V),∩,+) forms an algebraic lattice.

Proof

  1. [Closure] ∀U,W∈S,

    • U∩W∈S⟶  P-1.3.2, intersection of subspaces
    • U+W∈S⟶  P-1.4.1, smallest containing subspace

  2. [Commutativity] ∀U,W∈S,

    • U∩W=W∩U⟶  Commutativity of ∩
    • U+W={u+w:u∈U, w∈W}
      ={w+u:u∈U, w∈W}⟶  Def. 1.2.2.1, Commutativity of +

  3. [Associativity] ∀U,W,X∈S,

    • U∩(W∩X)=(U∩W)∩X⟶  Associativity of ∩
    • U+(W+X)={u+y: u∈U,y∈W+X}
      ={u+(w+x):u∈U, w∈W,x∈X}
      ={(u+w)+x:u∈U, w∈W,x∈X}
      ⟶Def. 1.2.3.1, Associativity of +
      =(U+W)+X

  4. [Idempotence] ∀U∈S

    • U∩U=U⟶  Idempotence of ∩
    • U+U=U⟶  P-1.4.1, smallest subspace containing U  is U itself

  5. [Absorption] ∀U,W∈S,

    • U+(U∩W)=U⟶ U∩W⊂U, P-1.4.1, smallest containing
       subspace
    • U∩(U+W)=U⟶ U⊂U+W, P-1.4.1 

Since all the 5 conditions for algebraic lattice are satisfied, according to P-1.5.3, (S,∩,+) is a valid algebraic lattice. q.e.d

2. Finite Dimensional Vector Spaces

It is only by the end of this chapter, that the title will make sense. Before understanding dimension, there is some build-up that needs to be done.

As usual, V(F)≡ (V,+,⋅,F) is a vector space over the field F≡(F,+^​,⋅^)

2.1. Span

Definition 2.1 [Linear Combination; Span]

1. [Linear Combination]
Given a finite list of vectors L=(v1​,v2​,⋯vn​), and scalars A=(a1​,a2​,⋯an​)∈Fn, linear combination of L with coefficients in A is:

u=a1​⋅v1​+a2​⋅v2​+⋯+an​⋅vn​≡i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​​

2. [Finite Spanning Set/Span]
Given a finite list of vectors from V, L=(v1​,v2​,⋯vn​), the finite spanning set or span(L) is the set of all possible linear combinations of L:

span(L)={i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​:ai​∈F∀i=1,2,⋯n}​

  • If span(L)=V, we say that L spans V.
  • vectors in L are called generators of span(L).

Properties of Span [P-2.1]

Given V(F)≡ (V,+,⋅,F) is a vector space over the field F≡(F,+^​,⋅^).

1. [Span is the smallest containing subspace]

Given a finite list of vectors L=(v1​,v2​,⋯vn​), span(L) is the smallest subspace that contains L.

Proof

There are two steps to this proof:

a. Proving that span(L) is a legitimate subspace of V
b. Proving that for any subspace U≤V, L⊂U⟹ span(L)⊂U

a. Span is a vector space

Firstly, ∀u∈ span(L), u∈V since ∃{ai​}i​∈F: u=∑i​ai​⋅vi​, by Definition 2.1.2 of span.
Since V is a vector space over F, by Definition 1.2.1, repeated application of closure, u∈V.

Hence, span(L)⊂V ⟶(s1)

Let u,w∈span(L).
Hence, ∃{ai​,bi​∈F}i​:

∴∴∴∴​u=i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​,w=i=1∑n​bi​⋅vi​u+w=i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​+i=1∑n​bi​⋅vi​u+w=i=1∑n​(ai​⋅vi​+bi​⋅vi​)⟶Def. 1.2.2.1, commutativity of +, repeatedu+w=i=1∑n​(ai​+^​bi​)⋅vi​⟶Definition 1.2.6.2, distributivityu+w∈span(L)⟶(s2)⟶Definition 2.1.2, span​

Given c∈F,

∴∴∴​c⋅u=c⋅i∑​ai​⋅vi​c⋅u=i=1∑n​c⋅(ai​⋅vi​)⟶Def. 1.2.6.1, Distributivity, repeatedc⋅u=i=1∑n​(c⋅^ai​)⋅vi​⟶Definition 1.2.3.2, associativity of ⋅c⋅u∈span(L)⟶(s3)⟶Definition 2.1.2, span​

From (s1), (s2), (s3), span(L) ⊂V and is closed under vector addition and scalar multiplication. Hence, by P-1.3.1, definition of subspace, span(L) is a bonafide subspace.

b. Span is smallest containing subspace

Let U≤V be an subspace s.t, L⊂U. By repeated application of closure properties, Definition 1.2.1, ∀{ai​∈F}i​,

i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​∈U

Hence, by definition 2.1.2, u∈span(L)⟹ u∈U. Hence, span(L)⊂U. q.e.d

2. [Adding/Removing Dependent Vector]

Given a list of vectors in V, call it L=(v1​,v2​,⋯vn​).

1. x∈span(L) iff span(L)=span(L∪{x})
2. Let x∈L. Then
x∈span(L−{x}) iff span(L) =span(L−{x})

i.e. Adding or Removing vectors already in the span of the rest (dependent vectors) preserves the span.

Proof

1. (⟹) Let x∈ span(L)

∴∴∴∴∴∴∴∴∴∴∴∴∴∴∴​x=i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​for some {ai​∈F}i​,Definition 2.1.2, spanLet u∈span(L), Then: u=i=1∑n​bi​⋅vi​⟶Definition 2.1.2, spanu=i=1∑n​(bi​+^​ai​+^​(−ai​))⋅vi​u=i=1∑n​(bi​+^​ai​)⋅vi​+i=1∑n​(−ai​)⋅vi​→Definition 1.2.6.1, distributivity &Def. 1.2.2.1, Commutativity of +, repeatedu=i=1∑n​(bi​+^​ai​)⋅vi​+i=1∑n​(−1F​⋅^ai​)⋅vi​⟶P-1.1.7, mult. with −1F​u=i=1∑n​(bi​+^​ai​)⋅vi​+i=1∑n​−1F​⋅(ai​⋅vi​)⟶Def. 1.2.3.2, associativity of ⋅u=i=1∑n​(bi​+^​ai​)⋅vi​+−1F​⋅i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​⟶Def. 1.2.6.1, distributivity, repeatedu=i=1∑n​(bi​+^​ai​)⋅vi​+−1F​⋅x∈span(L∪{x})span(L)⊂span(L∪{x})⟶(s1)​Let w∈span(L∪{x})w=i=1∑n​bi​⋅vi​+f⋅x⟶Def. 2.1.2, spanw=i=1∑n​bi​⋅vi​+f⋅i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​w=i=1∑n​bi​⋅vi​+i=1∑n​(f⋅^ai​)⋅vi​→Def. 1.2.6.1, distributivity, repeated &Def. 1.2.3.1, associativity of ⋅w=i=1∑n​(bi​+^​(f⋅^ai​))⋅vi​→Def. 1.2.2.1, commutativity of +, repeated& Def. 1.2.6.2, distributivityw∈span(L)⟶Def. 2.1.2, spanspan(L)⊃span(L∪{x})⟶(s2)​span(L)=span(L∪{x})⟶(s1) + (s2)​

(⟸) Let span(L)= span(L∪{x}). Take any u in both.

∴∴∴∴∴∴∴​u=i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​+r⋅x=i=1∑n​bi​⋅vi​s.t. r=0F​r⋅x=i=1∑n​bi​⋅vi​−i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​r⋅x=i=1∑n​bi​⋅vi​+−1F​⋅i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​⟶P-1.2.5, mult. with −1F​r⋅x=i=1∑n​bi​⋅vi​+⋅i=1∑n​(−1F​⋅^ai​)⋅vi​→Def. 1.2.6.1, distributivity repeated +Def. 1.2.3.2, associativity of ⋅r⋅x=i=1∑n​(bi​+^​(−1F​⋅^ai​))⋅vi​→Def. 1.2.2.1, commutativity repeated +Def. 1.2.6.2, distributivityx=i=1∑n​[r−1⋅^(bi​+^​(−1F​⋅^ai​))]⋅vi​→Def. 1.1.5.2, mult. inv. +Def. 1.2.6.2, distributivity, repeated +Def. 1.2.3.2, associativity of ⋅x∈span(L)⟶Def. 2.1.2, spanq.e.d​

2. Let x∈L, then this part follows from applying P-2.1.2.1 to L−{x}, hence

x∈span(L−{x})⟺span(L−{x})=span(L)q.e.d​

3. [Adding/Removing Independent Vector]

Given a list of vectors in V, call it L=(v1​,v2​,⋯vn​)

1. x∈/span(L) iff span(L)⊊ span(L∪{x})
2. Let x∈L, x∈/span(L−{x}) iff span(L−{x})⊊ span(L)

Proof

This property is a combination of two things:

  1. Contrapositive of P-2.1.2.
  2. Monotonicity of span, which is P-2.1.4.

Combining these two gives the result. q.e.d

4. [Monotonicity of Span]

Given A,B are two finite list of vectors from V,

A⊂B⟹span(A)⊂span(B)​

Proof

If A⊂B, we can write both as:

​A=(u1​,u2​,⋯un​)B=(u1​,u2​,⋯un​,x1​,x2​,⋯xm​)​

For any v∈span(A), v​=i=1∑n​ai​⋅ui​=i=1∑n​ai​⋅ui​+0V​⟶Def. 1.2.4.1 Add. identity=i=1∑n​ai​⋅ui​+i=1∑n​0F​⋅xi​⟶P-1.2.4, mult. with 0F​​∴v∈span(B)⟹span(A)⊂span(B)q.e.d

5. [Combination Laws of Span]

Given A,B are finite lists of vectors from V
1. span(A∪B)= span(A)+span(B)
2. span(A∩B)⊂ span(A)∩span(B)

Proof

1. P-2.1.4 gives you: ∴​span(A)⊂span(A∪B)span(B)⊂span(A∪B)​}​P-2.1.4,monotonicity of span​span(A)+span(B)⊂span(A∪B)⟶P-1.4.1, smallest containing subspace→(s1)​

Let x∈ span(A∪B)

∴∴∴∴​x=i:vi​∈A∪B∑​ai​⋅vi​⟶Def. 2.1.2, spanx=ui:vi​∈A∑​ai​⋅vi​​​+vi:vi​∈B−A∑​ai​⋅vi​​​u∈span(A),v∈span(B)⟶Def. 2.1.2, spanx∈span(A)+span(B)⟶Def. 1.4.1, subset sumspan(A∪B)⊂span(A)+span(B)→(s2)​

​∴By (s1), (s2),span(A∪B)=span(A)+span(B)q.e.d​

2. By P-2.1.4 ∴​span(A∩B)⊂span(A)span(A∩B)⊂span(B)​}​P-2.1.4,monotonicity of span​span(A∩B)⊂span(A)∩span(B)​

The reverse cannot be proved because, if x∈ span(A)∩span(B), then, ​x=i:vi​∈A∑​ai​⋅vi​=i:vi​∈B∑​bi​⋅vi​⟶Def. 2.1.2, spanx=i:vi​∈A∩B∑​ai​⋅vi​+i:vi​∈A−B∑​ai​⋅vi​=i:vi​∈A∩B∑​bi​⋅vi​+i:vi​∈B−A∑​bi​⋅vi​​

This need not necessarily belong to span(A∩B) since the terms from A−B or B−A can also be non-zero, hence, no conclusive way to show that x is in span(A∩B)

q.e.d

These properties are not random. They have an order in which they can appear.

1. [Synthesis]
In the subset sum definition, the simple question was that if given multiple subspaces, is a bigger vector space synthesizable such that it contains the the smaller ones.
The question here is simpler, given a list of vectors, what is the way to create a subspace containing all of them. The answer is span and the bonus is that it is the smallest containing vector space. This is covered in:

  • P-2.1.1 [Smallest Containing Subspace]

2. [Alteration]
View span as an operator. A function that takes in a list of vectors of arbitrary size and produces the smallest vector space containing them all. How will altering the list by one element alter the span, is the question.

  • P-2.1.2 [Adding/Removing Dependent Vectors]
  • P-2.1.2 [Adding/Removing Independent Vectors]

These two properties basically tell when the span is preserved, leading to the concept of dependent and independent vectors.
Span is preserved iff dependent vectors are added or removed while doing the same with indepedent vectors for sure alter the span, what relation the altered span will have comes about from P-2.1.4, which states that span is monotonic.

3. [Combination]
This is one level above alteration. Instead of altering list one vector at a time, the two ways in which two lists can be combined or have a relation is:

  1. Subset: P-2.1.4 [Monotonicity of Span]
  2. Union: P-2.1.5.1 [Span of Union]
  3. Intersection: P-2.1.5.2 [Span of Intersection]

These properties deal with that.

NOTE

All of these definitions and proofs are tailored for lists which are finite, hence the title of this chapter. For infinite lists, infinte linear combination, the definitions and tools are very different.

That comes under infinite dimensional linear algebra, which is totally different compared to what we are doing here.

2.2 Linear Independence [LI]

Definition 2.1 [Linear Independence (LI)]

Given a list of vectors L=(v1​,⋯vn​) from V, L is Linearly Independent (LI) if:

∃!{ai​∈F}i=1n​:0V​=i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​

Meaning, 0V​ is uniquely expressible as a lienar combination of the vectors in the list L.

Some common sense out of this:

  • If L is not LI, it is Linearly Dependent (LD)
  • The empty list ϕ=() is LI by default and span(ϕ)={0V​}.
  • In LI list, the unique linear combination is all zeros.
  • In LD list, a non-zero combination exists which add up to 0V​.
  • The all zeros combination is called trivial linear combination.

Properties of Linear Independence [P-2.2]

Given V(F)≡ (V,+,⋅,F) is a vector space over the field F≡(F,+^​,⋅^).

1. [Uniqueness of Coefficients]

Let L be a finite list of vectors from V,
L is LI ⟺ every vector in span(L) has unique coefficients of linear combination.

NOTE: This uniqueness implies a bijection between Fn & span(L).

Proof

(⟹) Let L be LI. Then, 0V​ is uniquely expressible as only the trivial linear combination, by Def. 2.2.

Take any x∈span(L). Suppose it can be expressed in two ways:

∴∴∴∴∴∴∴​x=i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​⟶Def. 2.1.2, span=i=1∑n​bi​⋅vi​x+(−x)=i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​+−i=1∑n​bi​⋅vi​0V​=i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​+−1F​⋅i=1∑n​bi​⋅vi​→Def. 1.2.5, add. inv.P-1.2.5, mult. with −1F​0V​=i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​+−1F​⋅(bi​⋅vi​)→Def. 1.2.6.1, Distribitivity &Def. 1.2.2.1, commutativity of + repeated0V​=i=1∑n​(ai​+^​(−1F​⋅^bi​))⋅vi​→Def. 1.2.3.2, Associativity of ⋅& Def. 1.2.6.2, distributivity0V​=i=1∑n​(ai​+^​(−bi​))⋅vi​→P-1.1.7, mult. with −1ai​+^​(−bi​)=0F​∀i=1,2,⋯n⟶Def. 2.2, LIai​=−(−bi​)=bi​∀i=1,2,⋯n⟶Def. 1.1.5.1, add inv.P-1.1.3.1, inverse of inverse ​

This means that any set of coefficients found to express x as linear combination of L is the set of coefficients and is unique.

(⟸) If every vector in span(L) has unique coefficients, since span is a subspace, by P-2.1.1, it should contain 0V​, Def. 1.2.4.1, additive identity.
Then, by what is given, 0V​ has is uniquely expressible, meaning L is LI from Def. 2.2.

Hence, generator is LI iff coefficients in span are unique. q.e.d

2. [Array Representation of Vectors]

Given any finite list L which is LI, has size n.

1. Any vector in span(L) can be represented by a unique array of numbers in Fn.
2. Operations + and ⋅ in span(L) are equivalent to :

  • ⊕:Fn×Fn ↦Fn
    (c1​,c2​,⋯cn​)⊕(d1​,d2​,⋯dn​)
    =(c1​+^​d1​,⋯cn​+^​dn​)

  • ⊙:F×Fn ↦Fn and ⊙:Fn ×F↦Fn
    c⊙(x1​,x2​,⋯xn​)= (x1​,x2​,⋯xn​)⊙c
    =(c⋅^x1​,⋯c⋅^xn​)

3. Fn(F) ≡(Fn,⊕,⊙,F) is a bonafide vector space.
4. span(L)≅Fn, i.e both are isomorphic to each other.

Proof

1. By P-2.2.1, since coefficients of span(L) is unique, and there are n coefficients in total. Hence, each vector in span(L) can be represented by a unique n-tuple from Fn.

2. Given x,y∈span(L), by P-2.2.2.1, we can find xF​,yF​ ∈Fn, which are their equivalent representations s.t.

x=∑i=1n​xF,i​⋅vi​y=∑i=1n​yF,i​⋅vi​​}P-2.2.2.1

x+y can be written as:

∴​x+y=i=1∑n​xF,i​⋅vi​+i=1∑n​yF,i​⋅vi​x+y=i=1∑n​(xF,i​+^​yF,i​)⋅vi​→Def. 1.2.2.1, commutativity of +& Def. 1.2.6.2, Distributivity​

Hence, the equivalent representation of x+y is : xF​⊕yF​ from the above.

c⋅x for any c∈F can be written as :

c⋅x​=c⋅i=1∑n​xF,i​⋅vi​=i=1∑n​(c⋅^xF,i​)⋅vi​→Def. 1.2.6.1, Distributivity &Def. 1.2.3.2, Associativity of ⋅​

Hence, the equivalent representation of c⋅x is : c⊙xF​ by the above.
∴⊕,⊙ are the equivalent of vector add. & scalar mult. in Fn space.

3. This part was actually mentioned in one of the examples below Definition-1.2, that Fn(F) is a bonafide vector space under the elmentwise add. and mult. operations.

4. To establish an isomorphism between span(L) and Fn, two conditions must be satisfied:

  1. Bijectivity: A one-to-one and onto mapping must exist between the two spaces.
  2. Linearity: The mapping must preserve vector addition and scalar multiplication, which was established in P-2.2.2.2.

By P-2.2.1, because L is linearly independent, every vector x∈span(L) can be uniquely represented by a coordinate vector xF​∈Fn. This uniqueness guarantees that the coordinate mapping ϕ:span(L)→Fn is injective (span(L)↪Fn).

Conversely, every coefficient vector xF​∈Fn maps to a vector in span(L) via the linear combination:

x=i=1∑n​xF,i​vi​

Because L is linearly independent, distinct coefficient vectors cannot map to the same vector in span(L). This establishes an injection in the opposite direction (Fn↪span(L)). Since the mapping is both well-defined for all inputs and injective, it constitutes a bijection.

Hence, isomorphism between the two vector spaces is established, span(L)≅Fn

q.e.d

3. [List with 0 vector is LD]

Given any finite list L which contains 0V​, it is LD

Proof

Let L=(v1​,v2​,⋯vn​) with v1​=0V​.

For any x∈span(L),

x​=a1​⋅0V​+i−2∑n​ai​⋅vi​=0V​+i−2∑n​ai​⋅vi​→P-1.2.4.1, mult. with 0V​=b1​⋅0V​+i−2∑n​ai​⋅vi​→such that a1​=b1​​

Which means x does not have a unique representation in span(L), hence by contrapositive of P-2.2.1, L is LD q.e.d

4. [Subset of LI & Superset of LD]

1. Given L is a finite LI list, any subset, T⊂L is also LI.
2. Given L is a finite LD list, any finite superset, T⊃L is also LD.

Proof

1. Let L be LI and T⊂L. For 0V​∈span(T), which will exist due to span being a subspace [P-2.1.1] and assume that T is LD :

​0V​=i:vi​∈T∑​ai​⋅vi​⟶Def. 2.1.2, span &Def. 2.2, non-trivial linear combination0V​=i:vi​∈T∑​ai​⋅vi​+i:vi​∈L−T∑​0F​⋅vi​→Def. 1.2.4.1, Additive Identity &P-1.2.4.2, multiplication with 0F​​

By P-2.2.1, uniqueness of coefficients guarantees that 0V​ as a linear combination of L can only be written as trivial linear combination.

Some ai​'s being non-zero breaks LI of L which is a contradiction to what is given.

Hence, T cannot be LD, it is LI. q.e.d

2. Let L be LD and T⊃L. For 0V​∈span(L), which will exist due to span being a subspace [P-2.1.1] : ​0V​=i:vi​∈L∑​ai​⋅vi​⟶Def. 2.1.2, span &Def. 2.2, non-trivial linear combination0V​=i:vi​∈L∑​ai​⋅vi​+i:vi​∈T−L∑​0F​⋅vi​→Def. 1.2.4.1, Additive Identity &P-1.2.4.2, multiplication with 0F​​

By Definition 2.2, a non-trivial linear combination of T exists which adds upto 0V​. Hence, T is LD. q.e.d

5. [Adding Vectors to Generator List]

Given L is an LI list of vectors from V

1. Adding an independent vector to L preserves LI.
2. Adding a dependent vector to L makes it LD.

Proof

1. Let x∈/span(L) with L given as LI is an independent vector.

Assume that L′=L∪{x} is LD. Hence, there exists a non-trivial linear combination {ai​}i=1n​ & c from F s.t.

​0V​=i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​+c⋅x⟶Def. 2.1.2, span​

Here, c has to be non-zero, otherwise the only choice for ai​'s will be all zeros, since L is LI [Definition 2.2].

∴∴∴∴​x=c−1⋅(−i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​)⟶Def. 1.2.5, additive inverseDef. 1.1.5.2, mult. inversex=(−c−1)⋅i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​→P-1.2.5, mult. w. −1F​Def. 1.2.3.1, associativity of +P-1.1.7, mult. with −1F​x=i=1∑n​(−c−1⋅^ai​)⋅vi​→Def. 1.2.6.1, Distributivity repeatedDef. 1.2.3.2, associativity of ⋅x∈span(L)→Def. 2.1.2, span​

This contradicts x being independent. Hence, L′ cannot be LD, and adding independent vector preserves LI. q.e.d

2. Say, x∈span(L).

∴∴∴​∃{ai​∈F}i=1n​:x=i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​⟶Def. 2.1.2, span−x=−1F​⋅x∈span(L)⟶P-1.2.5, mult. with −1F​Def. 1.2.1.2, closure under ⋅x+∈span(L)(−x)​​=0V​⟶Def. 1.2.5, additive inverse​

This means a non-trivial combination of L′ exists which adds upto 0V​, meaning, L′ is LD. q.e.d

6. [Linear Dependence Lemma]

1. A list of vectors from V, call it L =(v1​,⋯vn​) is LD iff ∃k∈N[n]:vk​∈ span(v1:k−1​) i.e. a vector exists in the list which is in the span of previous elements of the list.

2. In such a case, removing vk​ preserves the span, i.e. span(L)= span(L−{vk​}).

Proof

1. (⟹) Let L be an LD list of vectors. Hence, we can find {ai​}i​ not all zeros such that:

∴∴∴​0V​=i=1∑n​ai​⋅vi​⟶Def. 2.2, LDi∗≜i:ai​=0F​max​i⟶Last non-zero iai∗​⋅vi∗​=−i=i∗∑​ai​⋅vi​⟶Def. 1.2.2.1, commutativity of +Def. 1.2.5, additive inverseai∗​⋅vi∗​=−i=i∗∑​(−1F​⋅^ai​)⋅vi​→P-1.2.5, multiplication with −1F​Def. 1.2.6.1, DisributivityDef. 1.2.3.2, associativity of ⋅ai∗​⋅vi∗​=−i<i∗∑​(−1F​⋅^ai​)⋅vi​∈span(v1:i∗−1​)→by virtue of max.​

(⟸) Assume that ∃k:vk​∈ span(v1:k−1​), then:

∴∴∴​vk​=i=1∑k−1​ai​⋅vi​⟶Def. 2.1.2, span−vk​+i=1∑k−1​ai​⋅vi​=0V​→Def. 1.2.5, add. inv−1F​⋅vk​+i=1∑k−1​ai​⋅vi​=0V​→P-1.2.5, mult. with −1F​−1F​⋅vk​+i=1∑k−1​ai​⋅vi​+i=k+1∑n​0F​⋅vi​=0V​⟶Def. 1.2.4.1, add. identityP-1.2.4.2, mult. with 0F​​

So, we have a linear combination of L not all zeros, which adds upto zero, hence L is LD, by definition 2.2. q.e.d

2. Since vk​ is in span(v1:k−1​), then since span(v1:k−1​)⊂ span(L−{vk​}), by P-2.1.4, monotonicity of span, vk​ lies in the span of the rest.
Hence, by P-2.1.2.2, removing dependent vector, removing vk​ does not affect the span. q.e.d

This whole lemma can be converted into an algorithm

Algorithm 2.1 find_dependent(L)

Input

  • L is a list of vectors in V

Output

  • j:vj​∈span(v1:j−1​)

Algorithm

  1. if v1​=0V​ then
    1. return 1
  2. end if
  3. a∈Fn=in_span(0V​,L)
    Finds a list of non-trivial coefficients if they exist, else return all zeros list.
  4. if a=0 then
    1. return null
      L is LI, cannot find any non-trivial coefficients
  5. end if
  6. return i:ai​=0F​max​i
    The last non-zero coefficient is returned.

7. [Steinitz Exchange Lemma]

Given L=(v1​,⋯vn​) which spans V and S=(w1​,⋯wm​) which is LI. Then:

1. m≤n [any LI list cannot be longer than a spanning list]
2. ∃L′⊂L:∣L′∣=n−m and span(S∪L′)=V
[Can exchange vectors in L with those in S and preserve span].

Proof
The proof for this comes about algorithmically. Two operations are required for this :

  1. Span Membership in_span(x,L) checks if x is in the span of L by returning nontrivial coefficients (a1​,⋯an​)∈Fn if it does belong to the span else returns all-zeros.

  2. Dependence Removal find_dependent(L) will give the index j s.t. vj​∈span(v1:j−1​) if such a j exists, else returns null. This is algorithm 2.1.

Algorithm 2.2 [Steinitz Exchange Algorithm]

Input

  • L=(v1​,⋯vn​) is a list which spans V
  • S=(w1​,⋯wm​) is an LI list of vectors from V

Output

  • T is a list which spans V and contains all elements of S exchanged with elements of L and spans V

Algorithm

  1. T(0)←L
  2. for i=1 to m do
    1. R(i)=insert(wi​,T(i−1),i)
      This inserts wi​ as the i-th element in the list
    2. j←find_dependent(R(i))
    3. T(i)←R(i)−{rj(i)​}
      rj(i)​ is the j-th element in R(i)
  3. end for
  4. return T(m)

The algorithm's correctness will be proof of the theorem. Three things are required for the correctness:

  1. T(m) should span V
  2. T(m) should contain all the elements in S
  3. T(m) should have same length as L

Initialization : T(0)=L spans V is given.
Iterations: Suppose that T(i−1) spans V, then

​wi​=j=1∑n​aj(i−1)​⋅tj(i−1)​→tj(i−1)​≜j-th element in T(i−1)⟶Def. 2.1.2, span​

This combination will never be trivial since wi​ ∈V=span(T(i−1)) and since wi​ is from an LI list S, it cannot be zero as well, by P-2.2.3. Hence, no possibility of a trivial combination at all.

Step 2.1
After inserting wi​ as first element in the list, we have
R(i)=(t1(i−1)​,⋯ti−1(i−1)​,wi​⋯tn(i−1)​). This makes R(i) an LD list since adding a new vector from span of T(i−1) makes it LD, by P-2.2.5.2.

Step 2.2
This is the tricky part, because the way T(i) evolves is :

  1. R(1)={w1​,⋯}. find_dependent acting on this list will never remove w1​ since it does not belong to span(r1:0(i−1)​)= span(ϕ)={0V​}. Hence, T(1) will most definitely contain w1​.
  2. R(2)=(w1​,w2​,⋯). Again w1​ or w2​ cannot be removed since they come from an LI list S and by contrapositive of LD Lemma [P-2.2.6], it neither can belong to the span of the previous. Hence, T(2) contains both.

Doing this for i iterations, R(i)=(w1​⋯wi​⋯) and none of the first i elements can be removed due to contrapositive of LD Lemma, P-2.2.6. Hence, T(i) contains w1​ to wi​.

Step 2.3
By LD Lemma P-2.2.6.2, find_dependent will find a vector in R(i) which originally belonged to L and will only remove that, hence, exchanging a vector in L with one from S. The removal of that vector rj(i)​ will not affect the span and span of T(i) hence will still be V and will also have same length as L since step 2.2 inserts a vector and step 2.3 removes another. Hence,

  1. span(T(i))=V
  2. ∣T(i)∣=n

This hence, proves the correctness of algorithm 2.2, since T(m) will span V, contains all the elements of S and has length n.

Hence, through the correctness of algorithm 2.2, it is established that an alternative list L′ can be constructed such that is contains all elements of S and n−m elements of L with span (S∪L′)=V.

This proves P-2.2.7.2 provided that m≤n. That will be the case always because if m>n, then at some point, T(n) will become full of vectors from S and R(n+1)=T(n)∪{wn+1​} ⊂S should be LD by the above proof.
But that would mean P-2.2.4.2 [Subset of LI is LI] would not hold, hence, contradicting LI nature of S.

Hence, m≤n always, and exchange is possible via algorithm 2.2. q.e.d

There is a story to this as well, a much more deeper one that leads to something good.

1. [Uniqueness]
Span contains every possible linear combination. But a question arises, is the linear combination unique? When is it unique?
Answer: When Generator List is LI. This leads to properties:

  • P-2.2.1 : LI ⟺ Uniqueness of Coefficients
  • P-2.2.2 : Span of any LI list is isomorphic to Fn (F)

P-2.2.2 can also e stated as: the coefficients in the span of LI generators form a vector space themselves over the same field. This is the biggest takeaway from here, as it is what justifies representing vectors with a list of numbers.

Examples are:

  1. Vectors in physics denoted as a linear combination of i^, j^​, k^, the unit vectors in direction of x,y,z axes respectively. These vectors, they depend on how the the directions are chosen. Any three arbitrary directions can be chosen as x,y,z axes, but two sets of those axes are not the same. Their i^, j^​, k^ represent two very different directions in reality.

    But all these vectors may be represented using a tuple from R3, in which case, both those vector spaces with abstract unit direction vectors become isomorphic to R3.

  2. Solutions to the linear ODE y′′+y=0 form a 2-dimensional vector space (closed under addition and scalar multiplication). Two natural bases for this space are

    {cosx,sinx}and{eix,e−ix}.

    Under the first basis, the tuple (1,0) represents the function cosx; under the second, the same tuple (1,0) represents the function eix instead. There is no ambient coordinate grid here to fall back on, since the vectors themselves are functions — the tuple exists only because a basis was chosen, and a different choice of basis gives a different tuple for the same vector.

  3. The space P2​(F) of polynomials of degree at most 2 is 3-dimensional. The standard basis {1,x,x2} represents a polynomial as the tuple of its coefficients. But given three points x0​,x1​,x2​, the Lagrange basis {L0​,L1​,L2​}, where Li​ is the unique degree-2 polynomial with Li​(xj​)=δij​, represents the same polynomial as the tuple of its values at x0​,x1​,x2​. The two tuples for a given polynomial look nothing alike, yet both arise from the same isomorphism to F3, applied with two different bases.

  4. A length-n discrete signal lives in Rn (or Cn). The standard basis represents it as the tuple of its raw samples over time. The Fourier basis represents the same signal as the tuple of its frequency amplitudes. Here, switching between the two tuples — the same vector, viewed under two different bases — is the entire point of the exercise, since operations like filtering or compression are simple in one basis and opaque in the other.

  5. A qubit state lives in C2. Expressed in the computational basis {∣0⟩,∣1⟩}, a state is represented by one tuple; expressed in the Hadamard basis {∣+⟩,∣−⟩}, the same physical state is represented by a different tuple. This mirrors the i^,j^​,k^ example above, one level more abstract: two observers using different bases assign different coordinates to the same underlying state.

The punchline is : the tuples which are colloquially called vectors exist merely as representation of abstract vectors. The LI generators underlying them are usually hidden and the only thing that matters is what happens as the tuples are operated on.

This is where computational linear algebra starts. It always assumes the existence of some abstract vector space and all the vectors are represented and processed as tuple/array of numbers, without questioning their consequences in the abstract vector space. Only point of matter there is what happens in Fn.

2. [Alteration]
The question asked now is how does altering a generator list affect LI/LD?
This is covered by:

  • P-2.2.3, zero vector in a generator always makes it LD.
  • P-2.2.4, subset of LI is LI & superset of LD is LD.
  • P-2.2.5, adding independent vectors preserves LI & adding dependent vectors breaks it.

3. [Optimization]
For lists that span V, is it really necessary to have a huge list? Is it possible to reduce it somehow? This is covered by:

  • P-2.2.6, the LD Lemma along with Algorithm 2.1.
  • P-2.2.7, Steinitz Exchange Lemma and Algorithm 2.2.

NOTE

Applying LD Lemma, P-2.2.6 repeatedly to an LD list that spans V allows you to keep on reducing the generator list whilst preserving the spanning property of the list.
But it is Steinitz Lemma that puts a lower bound on how small the size of the list can get before it stops spanning V.

This leads to the concept of Minimal Spanning Set, i.e. the smallest set that spans V.

There is a second optimization problem that is equally interesting. Given an LI list, how much can it be extended before it becomes LD? The same two properties cover this as well.

NOTE

Applying P-2.2.5.1, adding independent vectors will expand the span and preserve LI. So, LI can be preserved whilst expanding the generator list.
But again it is Steinitz Lemma, P-2.2.7 that puts an upper bound on how big the size of the LI list can get.

This leads to the concept of Maximal LI Set in V.

The interesting thing is: the solution to both problems are a special class of generator lists known as : Basis, which is the topic of the next section.

2.3 Basis

Definition 2.3 [Finite Dim. Vector Spaces; Basis]

1. [FDVS] A vector space V(F) is Finite Dimensional if there exisits a finite generator list such that it span V.

2. [Basis] A basis of a vector space V(F) is a list of vectors which span V and are LI.

The existence of LI sets is not an issue. But the existence of spanning set may not always be guaranteed, which is why the concept of Finite Dimensional Vector Spaces [FDVS] comes in. It guarantees a finite generator list, which is also a spanning set.

This also guarantees the existence of the solution to both problems: Maximal LI set & Minimal Spanning Set. The solution to both is the special class of generators that are called the basis of V.

Multi-Layered Perceptron (MLP)

The Multi-Layered Perceptron (MLP) is the original Neural Network that flooded the market. This is the most basic topology in deep learning.

Things to know about any NN topologies in general:

  1. Any NN topology is by design meant to take in one specific type of data, from an input space X, transform it into another type of data that resides in the output space Y.

  2. The logic behind the transformation is what governs the equation of the forward pass [process of feeding input, getting output]. This is what is to be understood. The equations themselves are just embodiment of the design philosophy.

  3. Almost every NN topology can be broken down into layers which all come from a finite set of blueprints, just with different hyperparameters. For example, an MLP is basically a sequence of Linear + Activation layers, one unit (with different hyperparameters) repeating again and again.

The input space for an MLP is: Rdi​, i.e. space of real vectors, and the output space is some manifold, which lives in Rdo​, again a space of real vectors.

The point of a neural network is to capture or approximate any continuous vector valued function by tuning the parameters of the model from the data.

1. Basic Units of MLP

The basic units of an MLP are two layers, Linear & Activation layer. Each layer comes with two operations.

  1. Forward Pass: The actual transformation of input to output, y=f(x;θ).
  2. Backward Pass: Taking the gradient wrt output of a loss function, δy​ and computing:
    • Gradient wrt Parameters, δθ​
    • Gradient wrt Input, δx​

These two operations are present in every single layer, and by composition, every single NN topology. The purpose of layering is explained in the following subsection.

1.1. Loss Function

Before moving forward, the goal of any DL architecture is to provide a model blueprint which is able to learn a dataset D={(x(i),y(i))} efficiently.

The meaning of that is:

1. For all x(i)∈D0​ ={x(i)}, the output of the network y^​(i)=f(x(i);θ) should be close to the true value y(i)∈D1​ ={y(i)}. Some function measuring the closeness between two values in Y is required: l(⋅,⋅). As l(y^​(i),y(i))→0, closer the predictions on the training dataset are. The thing to be minimized is:

L(θ;D)≜∣D∣1​i∑​l(y^​(i),y(i))≡∣D∣1​i∑​l(i)

This is straight from the Empirical Risk Minimization framework, where l(⋅,⋅) serves as a measure of goodness of responses of the model on a single datapoint. L(θ;D) depends on (a) the parameters, (b) the dataset itself, which is assumed fixed.

Hence, the goal becomes:

θ∗=argθmin​L(θ;D)

2. The standard optimization methods like SGD, ADAM, RMSprop etc. use only first order information, viz the gradient of L(θ) wrt θ to update the parameters in a way that iteratively minimizes the Empirical Risk. Hence, to calculate ∇θ​L(θ). The efficiency part comes from here in the sense that, the algorithm to find the optimal θ∗ should be low in complexity.

3. Usually, transforms are chained in sequence, which translates to composing transforms. This is called layering, instead of evaluating in one go, the computation occurs in small steps. Only after the last layer will the loss function be evaluated. The concept is illustrated as follows (for one single datapoint):

​z0​z1​z2​...zL​lx​​​=x=f1​(z0​;θ1​)=f2​(z1​;θ2​)=fL​(zL−1​;θL​)=l(zL​,y)​

This is called a composition of transformations each with their own parameters θi​ i=1,2,...,L

The goal is to calculate gi​≡∇θi​​lx​ for all i=1,2,...,L. Suppose magically you obtain ∇zk​​lx​≡δzk​.

dzk​​=j∑​∂zk−1,j​∂fK​​dzk−1,j​+m∑​∂θk,m​∂fK​​dθk,m​→Total Derivitive Theorem=JfK​​(zk−1​)dzk−1​+JfK​​(θk​)dθk​​∴dlx​​=δzk⊤​dzk​→Total Derivitive Theorem=δzk⊤​JfK​​(zk−1​)dzk−1​+δzk⊤​JfK​​(θk​)dθk​=δzk−1⊤​dzk−1​+gk⊤​dθk​​∴​δzk−1​=JfK​​(zk−1​)⊤δzk​δθk​≡gk​=JθK​​(θk​)⊤δk​​

The last part is reexpressing total derivitive theorem in terms of zk−1​ & θk​

Hence, in one fell swoop, from δzk​, extracted δzk−1​ & ∇θi​​lx​, provided dependence of zk​ on zk−1​ & θk​ is known. Now one of those can be used to calculate the gradients up the chain. This is a recursive algo, which can be made iterative with a few clever tricks.

This recursive process of calculating derivitives is called backpropagation aka backprop or Backward Pass. The only thing missing is the base case, which is δzL​ usually hardcoded in autograd engines.

NOTE

This might as well be the most important part of this chapter. It is not the formulas, but the idea of using total derivitive theorem like this that is the star of the show.

This technique will be used again and again in many of the following chapters, getting this down at the start itself is better than struggling later.

1.2. Linear Layer

Forward Pass​

This is a fancy name for an affine linear transformation of the input vector.

Definition 1.1 [Forward Pass of Linear Layer]

x∈Rdi​, [di​-dim. vector] is the input. The Linear Layer transformation (aka Forward Pass) is:

​1. Forward Pass​: y=Wx+b​Where W∈Rdo​×di​ and b∈Rdo​are the parameters of this layer, theWeight Matrix and Bias Vector2. Time Complexity​: O(do​⋅di​)​For a single input, which comes from onematrix-vector product and vector addition.​

Geometrically, on the whole input space, this can be viewed as transforming the whole of Rdi​ into an affine subspace, which is b-shifted span of columns of W.

Backward Pass​

Given to you is ∂y∂l​ ≡δy​∈Rdo​.

The goal for the backpass of a linear layer is: Given δy​, compute ∂W∂l​≡ δW​∈Rdo​×di​, ∂b∂l​≡ δb​∈Rdo​ & ∂x∂l​≡δx​∈Rdi​. The first two are the parameter gradients, the last one is input gradient which will be passed to upstream layers.

Theorem 1.1 [Backpass of Linear Layer]

Linear Layer forward pass: y=Wx+b

  1. Given δy​∈Rdo​ (output gradient),

​1. δb​∈Rdo​=δy​2. δW​∈Rdo​×di​=δy​⋅x⊤3. δx​∈Rdi​=W⊤⋅δy​​​

  1. The total time complexity of the backpass is O(2⋅do​⋅di​)
Proof
1.1.1 The standard proof

​yi​=j∑​Wij​xj​+bi​​​⟶Def. 1.1.1, Linear Layer Fwd. Pass​​Observe that bi​ only affects yi​. Hence,​∴​∂bi​∂l​≡δb,i​=∂yi​∂l​⋅∂bi​∂yi​​=δy,i​δb​=δy​​

  • Much more elegent Matrix Calculus proof

∴​y=Wx+bdo​×1∂b∂l​​=do​×do​∂b∂y​​⋅do​×1∂y∂l​​​​⟶Def. 1.1.1, Linear Layer Fwd. Pass⟶ Chain rule ​Where, ∂b∂y​∈Rdo​×do​=[∂bj​∂yi​​](do​×do​)=[δij​]do​×do​​=Ido​​since bi​ only affects yi​, as given in forward pass,the off diagonal elements are all 0 and on diag.elements are 1.∴​δb​=Ido​​⋅δy​=δy​​q.e.d

1.1.2  Standard Proof

​yi​=j∑​Wij​xj​+bi​​​⟶ From def. of fwd pass​​Observe that Wij​ only affects yi​. Hence,​∂Wij​∂l​≡δW​[i,j]=∂Wij​∂yi​​⋅∂yi​∂l​=xj​δy,i​​​This is the formula for an outer product:δW​=δy​⋅x⊤​

  • Matrix Calculus

∴∴​y=Wx+bdy=dW⋅x+W⋅dx+dbdl=δy⊤​dy​​⟶Total Derivative​Since we need to calculate the effect of change in W to yand hence l, we shut off dx & db.∴∴∴∴​dl=δy⊤​⋅dW⋅xdl=tr(δy⊤​⋅dW⋅x)dl=tr(x⋅δy⊤​⋅dW)dl=tr(δW⊤​⋅dW)​​⟶ Cyclic Trace Identity​Hence, δW​=δy​⋅x⊤ q.e.d

1.1.3  Now just using matrix calculus. It is much more elegant and less cumber some to write.

∴​y=W⋅x+bdy=dW⋅x+W⋅dx+db​Since we are concerned with how x↦x+dxaffects y hence l, shut off all the other differentials.∴∴∴​dl=δy⊤​⋅dydl=δy⊤​⋅(W⋅dx)dl=δx⊤​⋅dx​∴δx​=W⊤δy​ q.e.d

1.2  Observe, in the three calculations above, only the second and third involve computing something new, and they are:

  • Outer product of (do​,),(di​,) which is O(do​⋅di​).
  • Matrix Vector Product of (di​,do​),(do​,1) which is O(do​⋅di​)

Hence, in total O(2⋅do​⋅di​). q.e.d

Proofs 1.1.1 & 1.1.2 show some nice tricks using matrix calculus to find derivatives. Neat'o. The trick is simple, if you need to find ∂h∂l​:

  • h is scalar, express dl as g⋅dh, δh​=g.

  • h is vector, express dl as g⊤dh, δh​=g.

  • h is matrix, express dl as tr(G⊤dh), δh​=G.

This is the crux of the whole matrix differentiation business. Notice that these are all some sort of inner products. For higher ranked tensors, express dl as a dot product of dh with something, say G. Then δh​=G.

Even in case of matrix, that trace equation, all you get is total derivative theorem packed in a convenient way with known matrix operations. Same for vector.

1.3 Activation Layer

If we just use linear layer, y=W⋅x+b, notice that all we are then doing is modeling the input output relation as a linear one. This is not always true for real world data, which is more often than not, nonlinear and very complex. Even C1 may not always be a valid assumption.

This is where activation functions come in, which are elementwise functions, that introduce nonlinearity into the mix. The general activation function is a nonlinear function ϕ:R↦R, which is applied elementwise to the output vector of the linear layer.

The activation functions are usually continuous, hence for a significant portion of the architectures, C1 relation between input and output is assumed, even though, in real life, it need not be like that.

Forward Pass​

Definition 1.2 [Activation Layer Forward Pass]

Given ϕ:R↦R, an activation function,

1. Forward Pass​: y=ϕ(x)∈Rd​

Forward pass on a vector x∈Rd
Where yi​=ϕ(xi​)∀i=0,1,...,d−1 (elementwise operation)

2. Time Complexity​: O(d)

Operation on x∈Rd has d elementwise calculations.

Backward Pass​

Theorem 1.2 [Activation Layer Backpass]

Activation Layer Forward Pass: y=ϕ(x)

  1. Given δy​∈Rd (output gradient)

δx​∈Rd=δy​⊙ϕ′(x)​​

  1. The time complexity is O(d)
Proof
1.1  Given δy​=∂y∂l​,

∴​y=ϕ(x)dy=ϕ′(x)⊙dx​​⟶Elementwise differential⟶(s1)​∴∴​dl=δy⊤​dydl=δy⊤​(ϕ′(x)⊙dx)​​⟶Total Derivative⟶ from (s1)​∴∴∴∴​dl=δy⊤​⋅diag(ϕ′(x))⋅dxdl=δx⊤​⋅dxδx​=diag(ϕ′(x))⋅δy​δx​=ϕ′(x)⊙δy​​​→Convert to Matmul form→Total Derivative→Diagonal matrix is its own transpose→Converting to Hadamard Product​ q.e.d

1.2 Since the calculation involves d derivative calculations and scalar products, time complexity is O(d)q.e.d

2. MLP Operations

This section deals with chaining Linear + Activation layer units, hence creating deep networks.

This is the actual MLP, which is a chain of different Linear + Activation transforms stacked together.

2.1 Forward Pass

Suppose you have multiple stacks of linear + activation layers, which is a composition of transforms.

⋮​Input​:a0​=xHidden Layer 1​:z1​=f1​(a0​)=W1​⋅a0​+b1​a1​=ϕ1​(z1​)Hidden Layer 2​:z2​=f2​(a1​)=W2​⋅a1​+b2​a2​=ϕ2​(z2​)Hidden Layer L​:zL​=fL​(aL−1​)=WL​⋅aL−1​+bL​aL​=ϕL​(zL​)​

One linear + activation layer is called a hidden layer. This sequence of computations is what is called an MLP. This can be written in the form of an algorithm, which is the Forward Pass for an MLP.

In essense, this is the repeated application of definition 1.1 & 1.2.

Algorithm 1.1 [MLP Forward Pass]

Input

  • x∈Rd0​, input to the network.
  • θ={Wi​,bi​}i=1L​, the parameters of the network.

Output

  • y∈RdL​, output of the network.

Algorithm

  1. a0​=x ∈Rd0​
  2. for i=1,2,…,L do
    1. zi​=Wi​⋅ai−1​+bi​∈Rdi​
      Definition 1.1.1, Linear layer forward pass
    2. ai​=ϕi​(zi​)∈Rdi​
      Definition 1.2.1, Activation layer forward pass
  3. end for
  4. return aL​

Given the output size of each layer is: d0​,d1​,d2​,…dL​, the time complexity for the forward pass of a single input is: O(∑i=0L−1​di​⋅di+1​), from definition 1.1.2 & definition 1.2.2.

2.2 Backward Pass

This section is the repeated application of theorem 1.1 & 1.2. From these, it is evident that for all linear + activation units, two things are required to be stored: ai−1​ & zi​, i.e. inputs to each linear and activation layers are required for the backpass.

A small tidbit, the ai​'s are called activations and zi​'s are called preactivations in MLP parlance.

Algorithm 1.2 [MLP Backpass]

Input

  • θ={Wi​,bi​}i=1L​, network parameters.
  • δaL​≡∂aL​∂l​∈RdL​, gradient wrt network output.
  • A={ai​}i=0L−1​ and Z={zi​}i=1L​, the relevant activations and preactivations respectively.
    If not cached during forward pass, recalculate it.

Output

  • δWi​=∂Wi​∂l​∈Rdi​×di−1​, i=1,2,…,L
  • δbi​=∂bi​∂l​∈Rdi​, i=1,2,…,L
  • δx​=∂x∂l​∈Rd0​

Algorithm

  1. Optional step if values not cached
    Calculate {zi​}i=1L​,{ai​}i=0L​ using Algorithm 1.1.
  2. for i=L,L−1,…,1 do
    1. Theorem 1.2.1, Activation layer backpass
      δzi​≡∂zi​∂l​=δai​⊙ϕi′​(zi​)∈Rdi​
    2. Theorem 1.1.1, Linear layer backpass
      δbi​≡∂bi​∂l​=δzi​∈Rdi​
    3. δWi​≡∂Wi​∂l​=δzi​⋅ai−1⊤​∈Rdi​×di−1​
    4. δai−1​=Wi⊤​⋅δzi​∈Rdi−1​
  3. end for
  4. return {δWi​,δbi​}i=1L​,δx​≡δa0​

The input gradient is required in case that itself is coming from some upstream layer, hence outside the scope of this algo, continuing the backprop upstream.

The time complexity of backprop on whole MLP is: O(∑i=0L−1​di​⋅di+1​) from theorem 1.1.2 & theorem 1.2.2.

3. Batching in MLP

The previous section detailed the two fundamental operations of any NN, forward & backward pass for MLP with single input. But the dataset D has N datapoints.

One option is, you can loop over all the datapoints and calculate the outputs, then gradients and proceed, but from an implementation PoV, it would become very tedious and would not utilize the hardware fully.

The interesting thing to note is, multiple forward passes through linear and activation layer can be converted into a single matrix multiplication.

Definition 1.3 [Batched Forward Pass]

1. Linear Layer​

Given input vectors x(i)∈Rd0​'s in the form of a matrix X∈RN×d0​ as follows:

X=​−−−​x(0)⊤x(1)⊤⋮x(N−1)⊤​−−−​​∈RN×d0​

and the parameters for the linear layer being W∈Rd1​×d0​ & b∈Rd1​ as usual, the forward pass for a whole batch is:

Y=X⋅W⊤+b​b⊤∈RN×d1​​

+b​ is broadcasted addition, and the time complexity of this operation is
O(N⋅i=0∑L−1​di​⋅di+1​)​

2. Activation Layer​

Given input vectors x(i)∈Rd0​'s in the form of a matrix X∈RN×d0​ as follows:

X=​−−−​x(0)⊤x(1)⊤⋮x(N−1)⊤​−−−​​∈RN×d0​

and ϕ being the elementwise activation function, the forward pass in batch becomes: Y=ϕ(X)∈RN×d0​​

The time complexity of this operation is
O(N⋅d0​)​

Notice that the time complexity is the same as that of looping over datapoints individually. But current hardware is extremely efficient in performing matrix multiplications, which makes this method much more efficient for the linear layer and trivially parallelzable for activation layer.

3.1 Batched Backprop

Remember from Section 1.1 that the Empirical Risk for the whole dataset can be written as:

​L(θ;D)=∣D∣1​⋅i=0∑N−1​l(y^​(i),y(i))y^​(i)≡f(x(i);θ)​

where f(x;θ) is the MLP model, whose parameters θ is to be optimized such that

θ∗=argθmin​L(θ;D)

i.e. Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM).

Again, for using first order methods like SGD, RMSProp, ADAM etc, ∇θ​L(θ;D) has to be calculated. By linearity of the ∇ operator:

∇θ​L(θ;D)=∣D∣1​⋅i=0∑N−1​∇θ​l(y^​(i),y(i))

∇θ​l(y^​(i),y(i)) is calculated using backpropagation for a single datapoint.

Brute forcing this, the naive algorithm would be:

Naive Batched Backpass

  1. g=0, the accumulator of gradients.
  2. for i=0,1,…,N−1 do
    1. y^​(i)=f(x(i);θ), forward pass using Algorithm 1.1 in Section 2.1.
    2. l(i)=l(y^​(i),y(i)), calculate loss for one datapoint.
    3. Calculate δθ(i)​, i.e. gradients for the i-th datapoint using Algorithm 1.2 in Section 2.2.
    4. g+=δθ(i)​
  3. end for
  4. return g/∣D∣

Towards a better, more efficient way of calculating backpass in terms of matrix multiplications:

The overall procedure of backpropagation remains the same: find output gradients first, which in batched case is AL​∈RN×dL​, so the corresponding gradient is: δAL​ ≡∇AL​​L(θ;D).

∴​AL​=​−−−​aL(0)⊤​aL(1)⊤​⋮aL(N−1)⊤​​−−−​​∈RN×dL​L(θ;D)=∣D∣1​⋅i=0∑N−1​l(y^​(i),y(i))=∣D∣1​⋅i=0∑N−1​l(aL(i)​,y(i))∇AL​​L(θ;D)=∣D∣1​⋅i=0∑N−1​∇AL​​l(aL(i)​,y(i))∇AL​​L(θ;D)=∣D∣1​⋅i=0∑N−1​​−−−​∇aL(0)​​l(aL(i)​,y(i))⊤∇aL(1)​​l(aL(i)​,y(i))⊤⋮∇aL(N−1)​​l(aL(i)​,y(i))⊤​−−−​​∈RN×dL​​

The last step comes about by expanding the gradient. This is where the magic happens since:

∇aL(j)​​l(aL(i)​,y(i))={0∇aL(i)​​l(aL(i)​,y(i))​i=ji=j​

effectively, for i-th matrix in summation, only its i-th row is non-zero, rest all rows are zeroed out. This simplifies the calculations:

∇AL​​L(θ;D)​=∣D∣1​⋅​−−−​∇aL(0)​​l(aL(0)​,y(0))⊤∇aL(1)​​l(aL(1)​,y(1))⊤⋮∇aL(N−1)​​l(aL(N−1)​,y(N−1))⊤​−−−​​∈RN×dL​=∣D∣1​⋅​−−−​∇aL(0)​​l(0)⊤∇aL(1)​​l(1)⊤⋮∇aL(N−1)​​l(N−1)⊤​−−−​​​​

Each row is an output gradient given for an individual backpass.

KEY TRICK

This is not just true for AL​, it is true for any Al​, Zl​, i.e each row of the gradient matrix is the gradient from an individual backpass, which is why the gradient matrices will have N rows for activations, preactivations and input.

This happens since the computations due to zl(j)​ cannot affect l(aL(i)​,y(i)), i.e computations due to the j-th datapoint cannot affect the loss of the i-th datapoint, so any variable appearing in the computation sequence of j-th datapoint like zL(j)​ or aL(j)​ will not affect the i-th loss l(i).

This follows from the implicit assumption of ERM, which states that datapoints are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d).

This is the key trick underneath all the mathematics. The only catch is: the output gradient matrix must be given or should be calculable somehow.

This leads to better version of batched backpass. First going layer wise:

Theorem 1.3 [Batched Backprop of Linear & Activation Layer]

1. Linear Layer​

Given Y=X⋅W⊤+b​b⊤ the forward pass for linear layer [Definition 1.3.1], and δY​=∇Y​L(θ;D)∈RN×d1​:

δY​=∣D∣1​​−−−​δy(0)⊤​δy(1)⊤​⋮δy(N−1)⊤​​−−−​​=∣D∣1​​−−−​∇y(0)​l(0)⊤∇y(1)​l(1)⊤⋮∇y(N−1)​l(N−1)⊤​−−−​​

Then,

​1. δb​=sum(δY​,axis = 0)∈Rd1​2. δW​=δY⊤​⋅X∈Rd1​×d0​3. δX​=δY​⋅W∈RN×d0​​​

The sum operation denotes a row sum (axis = 0).

2. Activation Layer​

Given Y=ϕ(X) from [Definition 1.3.2] and δY​ =∇Y​L(θ;D) ∈RN×d1​,

δX​=δY​⊙ϕ′(X)∈RN×d1​​

Proof
1. Linear Layer​
Y=X⋅W⊤+b​b⊤⟶ Definition 1.3.1, Batched Forward Pass, Linear LayerY=X⋅W⊤+1N​⋅b⊤⟶Rewrite as an outer product∴dY=dX⋅W⊤+X⋅dW⊤+1N​⋅db⊤∴​dL=tr(δY⊤​dY)=tr(δY⊤​⋅dX⋅W⊤)+tr(δY⊤​⋅X⋅dW⊤)+tr(δY⊤​⋅1N​⋅db⊤)=tr(W⊤⋅δY⊤​⋅dX)+tr(δY⊤​⋅X⋅dW⊤)+tr(δY⊤​⋅1N​⋅db⊤)⟶Cyclic property of trace=tr(W⊤⋅δY⊤​⋅dX)+tr(dW⋅X⊤⋅δY​)+tr(δY⊤​⋅1N​⋅db⊤)⟶tr(A⋅B)=tr(B⊤⋅A⊤)=tr(W⊤⋅δY⊤​⋅dX)+tr(X⊤⋅δY​⋅dW)+tr(δY⊤​⋅1N​⋅db⊤)⟶Cyclic property of trace=tr(W⊤⋅δY⊤​⋅dX)+tr(X⊤⋅δY​⋅dW)+tr(db⋅1N⊤​⋅δY​)⟶tr(A⋅B)=tr(B⊤⋅A⊤)=tr(W⊤⋅δY⊤​⋅dX)+tr(X⊤⋅δY​⋅dW)+tr(1N⊤​⋅δY​⋅db)⟶Cyclic property of trace​

Hence, by total derivitive theorem for matrices:

​δb​=δY⊤​1N​=sum(δY​,axis = 0)δW​=δY⊤​XδX​=δY​⋅Wq.e.d​

2. Activation Layer​

Y=ϕ(X)⟶Definition 1.3.2, Batched Forward Pass, Activation Layer∴∴​dY=ϕ′(X)⊙dXdL=tr(δY⊤​⋅(ϕ′(X)⊙dX))​The next step is to use the identity: tr(A⊤(B⊙C))=tr((A⊙B)⊤C)∴∴​dL=tr((δY​⊙ϕ′(X))⊤⋅dX)δX​=δY​⊙ϕ′(X)⟶Total Derivitive Theoremq.e.d​

3.2 Batched MLP Algorithms

Previous section dealt with batched bacpass for the two layers. Now to convert them into algos:

Algorithm 1.3 [MLP Batched Forward Pass]

Input

  • X∈RN×d0​, input to the network.
  • θ={Wi​,bi​}i=1L​, the parameters of the network, as usual.

Output

  • Y∈RN×dL​, output of the network.

Algorithm

  1. A0​=X ∈RN×d0​
  2. for i=1,2,…,L do
    1. Definition 1.3.1, Linear layer batched forward pass
      Zi​=Ai−1​⋅Wi⊤​+b​bi⊤​∈RN×di​
    2. Definition 1.3.2, Activation layer batched forward pass
      Ai​=ϕi​(Zi​)∈RN×di​
  3. end for
  4. return AL​

The time complexity of this forward pass as discussed is still the same as the naive version, i.e. O(∑i=0L−1​di​⋅di+1​), but the improvement is that on hardware this is the version that will run faster due to hardware for efficient matmuls being present.

Algorithm 1.4 [MLP Batched Backpass]

Input

  • θ={Wi​,bi​}i=1L​, network parameters.
  • δAL​≡∂AL​∂L​∈RN×dL​, gradient wrt network output.
  • A={Ai​}i=0L−1​ and Z={Zi​}i=1L​, the relevant activations and preactivations respectively.
    If not cached during forward pass, recalculate it.

Output

  • δWi​=∂Wi​∂L​∈Rdi​×di−1​, i=1,2,…,L
  • δbi​=∂bi​∂L​∈Rdi​, i=1,2,…,L
  • δX​=∂X∂L​∈RN×d0​

Algorithm

  1. Optional step if values not cached
    Calculate {Zi​}i=1L​,{Ai​}i=0L​ using Algorithm 1.3.
  2. for i=L,L−1,…,1 do
    1. Theorem 1.3.2, Activation layer batched backpass
      δZi​≡∂Zi​∂L​=δAi​⊙ϕi′​(Zi​)∈RN×di​
    2. Theorem 1.3.1, Linear layer batched backpass
      δbi​≡∂bi​∂L​=sum(δZi​,axis=0)∈Rdi​
    3. δWi​≡∂Wi​∂L​=δZi⊤​⋅Ai−1​∈Rdi​×di−1​
    4. δAi−1​=δZi​⋅Wi​∈RN×di−1​
  3. end for
  4. return {δWi​,δbi​}i=1L​,δX​≡δA0​

And this concludes the chapter on MLP.

Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)

These are a family of NNs specifically designed to deal with images.
The core novelty of CNNs comes from the convolutional layer whose input space X=Rci​×hi​×wi​, which is images with ci​ channels, width and height hi​ & wi​ respectively.
The output space is Y=Rco​×ho​×wo​ which is also an image with different channels and dimensions.
